sections.title: WHO are the main characters in this story?
prompts: Who were the orphans at Goldie Leigh? What words would you use to describe their lives? (Think: vulnerable, isolated)
Who were the doctors treating ringworm? Were they kind or cruel? Can someone cause harm while trying to help?
Who were the hospital staff during the war? What kind of people were they? (Think: resilient)
Who were the children admitted in 1961? How were they treated by society?

sections.title: What was IMPORTANT to them? Why?
prompts: What was important to the orphans? Did they just need a roof, or did they need something more? What was missing from their lives?
What was important to the doctors? Why did they choose radiation as a treatment? What were they trying to achieve?
What was important to the hospital staff during the Blitz? Why did they form a Home Guard company instead of just leaving?
Why was it important that the children with learning difficulties had somewhere to go in 1961? What would have happened to them otherwise?

sections.title: What was the BLOCKER?
prompts: What stopped the orphans from having a normal childhood? Was it just money, or something else?
What was the blocker for treating ringworm safely? Why could doctors not just use medicine? (Think about what had and had not been invented yet.)
What was the blocker during the war? What specific weapon made it impossible to stay?
What was the blocker for children with learning difficulties? Was it a medical problem or a society problem?

sections.title: What was the FIX or solution?
prompts: How did the Woolwich Union try to fix the problem of orphaned children? Did it actually work, or did it create new problems?
How did doctors try to fix ringworm? What was the unprecedented treatment? Did the fix turn out to be worse than the problem?
How did the hospital fix the danger from V2 rockets? What did evacuated actually look like in practice?
How did the NHS try to fix things after 1961? Has society got better at looking after vulnerable people since then?

sections.title: What happened in the END?
prompts: What happened to the children who had radiation? What does iatrogenic mean and why is it such an important word?
What happened to Goldie Leigh Hospital in 1988?
What is on the site today? Who uses it now?
How do you think the people who were treated there as children felt when they found out the radiation had harmed them?

sections.title: What happened NEXT? What should happen next?
prompts: What lessons did the medical profession learn from places like Goldie Leigh?
How do doctors test treatments today to make sure they are safe? (Think about clinical trials.)
Do you think there should be a memorial or plaque at Goldie Leigh for the children who were harmed? Why or why not?
You are visiting this place on Tuesday. What will you look out for? What questions would you ask if you could talk to someone who worked there?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who were the most important people in this story? Describe them using adjectives.
What kind of person was Richard Duke of Gloucester? How would you describe his character?
How would you describe Henry VIII based on how he treated Anne Boleyn?
What words would you use to describe Guy Fawkes and his role in the conspiracy?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What did William the Conqueror want when he built the Tower, and why was it important?
Why was the throne so important to Richard Duke of Gloucester?
What did Guy Fawkes and his fellow conspirators believe was worth risking their lives for?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What stood in the way of Richard becoming king?
What blocked Anne Boleyn from saving herself at her trial?
What went wrong for Guy Fawkes and the Gunpowder Plot conspirators?

sections.title: What was the Fix/solution?
prompts: How did Richard deal with the problem of the two princes?
How did Henry VIII solve his "problem" with Anne Boleyn?
How did the authorities deal with Guy Fawkes after he was captured?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: What happened to the princes in the Tower?
What was Anne Boleyn's fate?
What happened to Guy Fawkes and the other plotters?
What is the Tower of London used for today?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: What discovery was made beneath a staircase nearly two hundred years later?
How is the Gunpowder Plot remembered today?
Why do you think the Tower is now one of London's biggest tourist attractions?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who were the key people or groups in this story? Describe them using adjectives.
What kind of leader was King Charles II? How would you describe his decision to leave London?
How would you describe William Mompesson, the rector of Eyam?
What words would you use to describe the ordinary people of London who were left behind?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What mattered most to the authorities who painted red crosses on doors?
What was most important to the villagers of Eyam when they chose to quarantine themselves?
Why was it important for the Bills of Mortality to be published each week?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What made it so difficult to stop the plague from spreading?
Why did the remedies people tried not work?
What prevented the poor from escaping the city like the wealthy?

sections.title: What was the Fix/solution?
prompts: What precautions did the authorities try to put in place?
What was Eyam's solution to prevent the plague spreading further?
What unexpected event may have finally helped end the plague in London?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: How many people are estimated to have died during the Great Plague?
What happened to the village of Eyam?
How did the Great Fire of 1666 change London?
Did the plague ever return to London on the same scale?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: How did the Great Fire change the way London was rebuilt?
What do you think people learned about dealing with epidemics?
Can you think of any connections between the Great Plague and more recent outbreaks of disease?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who were the patients in Bedlam? What adjectives would you use to describe them?
Who were the spectators? What kind of people visited?
Who were the reformers? What adjectives describe their character?
Who were the staff and "basket men"? How would you describe their behaviour?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What was important to the hospital managers? Why did they keep the doors open to visitors?
What was important to the reformers? Why did they fight so hard for change?
What mattered to the spectators? Why did they keep coming back?
What was important to the patients? What did they desperately need?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What stopped the patients from getting proper treatment for so long?
What made it difficult for reformers to change things?
Why did ordinary people not speak up sooner?
What role did money play as a blocker to change?

sections.title: What was the Fix?
prompts: Who started pushing for change, and what did they demand?
What specific improvements were made to the hospital?
How did new laws help protect patients?
How did public attitudes towards mental illness begin to shift?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: What happened to the public viewing galleries?
How are patients treated at Bethlem Royal Hospital today?
What does the word "bedlam" mean in modern English?
How does the author feel about the way society behaved? How can you tell?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: How do you think society's view of mental health has changed since Bedlam?
Do you think people are still "spectators" of other people's suffering today? How?
What lessons should we learn from the story of Bedlam?
If you could go back in time, what would you say to the people who visited Bedlam?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who were the first-class passengers? What adjectives would you use to describe their lives on board?
Who were the steerage passengers? How would you describe their situation?
Who was Captain Edward Smith? What kind of leader was he?
Who were the band members? What adjectives describe their character?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What was important to the White Star Line? Why did they boast about the ship?
What was important to the steerage passengers? What were they hoping for?
What mattered to the first-class passengers on the night of the sinking?
What was important to the band members as the ship went down?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What stopped the ship from avoiding the iceberg?
What blocked the steerage passengers from reaching the lifeboats?
Why was the Californian unable to help, even though it was nearby?
What prevented there from being enough lifeboats for everyone?

sections.title: What was the Fix?
prompts: How did maritime law change after the Titanic disaster?
What was the International Ice Patrol and why was it created?
How did the rules about radio operators change?
How did the disaster change the way ships were designed?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: How many people died, and how did most of them die?
What happened to the people in the lifeboats?
Where is the Titanic now?
How does the author want us to feel about what happened?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: How do you think the survivors felt in the days and weeks after the disaster?
Do you think something like the Titanic disaster could happen today? Why or why not?
What lessons should we learn from the Titanic?
If you could change one thing about that night, what would it be and why?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who were the two rival families, and what were they like?
Describe Jennet Device. How old was she, and what kind of situation was she in?
Who was Roger Nowell, and what adjectives would you use to describe him?
How would you describe the relationship between the families and their neighbours?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What was important to Roger Nowell, and why did he want to find witches?
What was important to the villagers of Pendle?
Why was survival so important to the Demdike and Chattox families?
Why might Jennet have felt it was important to do what the adults asked her to do?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What made life so dangerous for the families of Pendle Hill?
How did superstition block people from seeing the truth?
What stopped the accused from defending themselves properly?
Why could nobody stop the trials once they had started?

sections.title: What was the Fix or solution?
prompts: What did the prosecution believe was the "fix" for the problem of witchcraft?
How did using Jennet as a witness help the prosecution get what they wanted?
Was this really a fair solution? Why or why not?
What would a truly fair solution have looked like?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: What was the verdict, and what happened to the ten accused people?
How do you think Jennet felt after the trial was over?
What happened to Jennet twenty-one years later?
Why is the ending of this story ironic (the opposite of what you would expect)?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: What do you think life was like for Jennet after her own accusation?
Did the witch trials in England eventually stop? What might have caused that?
How do you think this story changed the way people thought about children giving evidence?
Could something like this happen today? Why or why not?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who found the Tollund Man, and what were they doing at the time?
Who was the Tollund Man? Describe what he looked like when he was discovered.
Who was P.V. Glob, and what adjectives would you use to describe his work?
Who else is involved in this story - think about the people who may have killed him 2,400 years ago.

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What was important to the Iron Age people who may have sacrificed the Tollund Man?
Why was it important to the archaeologists to excavate the body carefully?
Why is the Tollund Man important to scientists today?
What was so important about his last meal?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What makes it so difficult for scientists to solve this murder mystery?
Why can we never know for certain whether the Tollund Man was a sacrifice or a criminal?
What evidence has been lost or destroyed over 2,400 years?
What are the limits of forensic science when dealing with something this ancient?

sections.title: What was the Fix or solution?
prompts: How have scientists used forensic analysis to learn more about the Tollund Man?
How did the discovery of other bog bodies across Europe help build a picture of what happened?
What technologies have been used to study the body?
Has science given us a definite answer, or just better guesses?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: Where is the Tollund Man now, and how is he displayed?
What is the most widely accepted theory about his death?
How do visitors react when they see his face in the museum?
Why does the author say "the gap between the ancient world and our own seems to vanish"?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: What other bog bodies have been discovered since the Tollund Man?
How might future technology help solve this mystery?
What has the Tollund Man taught us about Iron Age societies?
Do you think we will ever know the full truth about how and why he died?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who were Burke and Hare? Describe them using at least three adjectives.
What kind of person was Dr Robert Knox? What words would you use to describe his attitude?
Who were the victims? What do we know about the kind of people they were?
If you had to pick one word to describe each main character, what would it be and why?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What did Burke and Hare care about most? How do you know?
What was important to the physicians and anatomists? Why did they need bodies so desperately?
Why was money such a powerful motivator in this story?
Did anyone in this story care about doing the right thing? Explain your answer.

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What was the main problem that started the whole body-snatching trade?
What obstacles did the Resurrection Men face when stealing bodies?
What finally blocked Burke and Hare from continuing their murders?
Why was it so difficult for the prosecution to build a case against both men?

sections.title: What was the Fix or solution?
prompts: How did the prosecution solve the problem of not having enough evidence?
What deal was Hare offered, and why did it work?
How did Parliament try to fix the wider problem after the trial?
Do you think the Anatomy Act was a good solution? Why or why not?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: What happened to William Burke?
What happened to William Hare and Dr Knox?
What happened to Burke's body after his execution?
How did the public react to the whole affair?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: How did the Anatomy Act of 1832 change things for medical schools?
Why do you think Burke's skeleton is still on display today?
Could something like this happen in modern times? Why or why not?
What lessons can we learn from this story about greed and morality?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who were the ordinary people of London during the Blitz? Use at least three adjectives to describe them.
Describe the children who were evacuated. What were they feeling?
Who were the heroes of the Blitz? Think about firefighters, wardens and ambulance drivers.
Who was the enemy? What kind of leader was Hitler in this story?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What mattered most to the families sheltering in the Underground?
Why was morale so important during the Blitz?
What was important to Hitler? Why did he believe the bombing would work?
What did the evacuated children care about most?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What was the biggest threat to the people of London?
What obstacles did evacuated children face in the countryside?
What made the Balham station disaster so shocking?
Why could the British government not simply stop the bombing?

sections.title: What was the Fix or solution?
prompts: How did people protect themselves during the bombing?
How did the Underground stations become shelters, even though the government said no?
What role did community spirit play in surviving the Blitz?
What eventually stopped the Blitz?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: When and why did the Blitz finally stop?
How many people were killed and how many homes destroyed?
What did children find when they returned from evacuation?
Did Hitler achieve his goal? Explain your answer.

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: How do you think London rebuilt itself after the Blitz?
Why is the "Blitz spirit" still talked about today?
How might the experience of evacuation have affected children for the rest of their lives?
Could something like the Blitz happen in London today? Why or why not?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who was Frank Morris and what kind of person was he? What adjectives best describe his personality?
Who were the Anglin brothers and what role did they play?
Who was Al Capone and why was he important to the Alcatraz story?
What about the guards and the FBI - how would you describe them?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What did Morris and the Anglins want more than anything, and why?
Why was freedom so important to them that they risked their lives?
What was important to the authorities and why did they care so much about keeping prisoners locked up?
Why did the FBI care about proving the men had drowned?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What physical barriers stood between the prisoners and freedom?
How did the constant surveillance make escape seem impossible?
What natural dangers surrounded the island?
Why had every previous escape attempt failed?

sections.title: What was the Fix/solution?
prompts: How did they solve the problem of getting out of their cells?
How did they solve the problem of the nightly bed checks?
How did they plan to cross the dangerous water?
What materials did they use and where did they get them?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: What did the guards find the next morning?
What happened during the massive search?
What was the FBI's official conclusion and do you agree with it?
What happened to Alcatraz prison itself?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: What was the mysterious 2013 letter and what did it claim?
Why couldn't experts agree on whether the letter was real?
What is Alcatraz used for today?
Why do you think this mystery still fascinates people over sixty years later?

sections.title: Who are the main characters?
prompts: Who was Captain Benjamin Briggs? What kind of man does he seem to have been?
Who else was on board and what do we know about them?
Who was Captain Morehouse and what was his role in the story?
How would you describe the Mary Celeste herself - can a ship be a "character"?

sections.title: What was Important to them?
prompts: What was important to Captain Briggs about this voyage?
Why was the safety of his family and crew his responsibility?
Why was it important to the Dei Gratia crew to investigate the drifting ship?
Why was solving this mystery important to the authorities?

sections.title: What was the Blocker?
prompts: What problem or danger might have forced the crew to abandon a perfectly good ship?
Why couldn't investigators work out what happened?
What made this mystery so much harder to solve than a normal crime?
Why did none of the theories fully explain all the evidence?

sections.title: What was the Fix/solution?
prompts: What is the most plausible theory and why does it make sense?
How does the alcohol fumes theory explain the missing lifeboat, the rope, and the panic?
Why hasn't this theory been proven beyond doubt?
Has anyone come up with a better explanation? What do YOU think happened?

sections.title: What happened in the End?
prompts: What happened to the ten people on board? Were they ever found?
What happened to the Mary Celeste after she was recovered?
How did the investigation end - was it satisfying?
What was the official conclusion?

sections.title: What happened Next?
prompts: How did the story of the Mary Celeste become so famous?
Why do people still talk about it over 150 years later?
Do you think we will ever know the truth?
If you were a detective, what would you look for to solve this case?
